Title: NST Penang former stringer dies
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Former New Straits Times Penang reporter Paul Toh Huat Hock, 77, has died.
Toh breathed his last on Sunday and was cremated today.
Toh's former colleagues remember him as one of the best contributors.
K. Kandiah, 74, a former sports contributor, said he was shocked to receive news about Toh's passing.
"Despite having left our reporting job, we continued to remain friends.
"The last time I spoke to him was during Chinese New Year when we caught up over the good old days. He didn't mention anything about his health and seemed normal and fine to me," he said.
Another former colleague, Predeep Nambiar, who has since left the company, said he was saddened when he got news of Toh's passing.
"Paul Toh, as far as I can recall, served the New Straits Times as a stringer sometime in 2013.
"He was brought in by the then Bureau Chief, Sharanjit Singh, to help us cover assignments that we were not able to cover.
"At the time, the Streets section was heavily read by Penangites and was one of two local story sources you could read on anything Penang.
"Toh tirelessly covered many events in a day, and never once complained. As a retiree, he would say, 'I can use every ringgit I can get to put food on the table.'
"In return, he gave us quality written English with flair, the kind you expect from old-timer journalists like him. He did the social beat, covering retail and hotel-related events," he said.
Predeep said Toh had left a mark on NST, especially the bureau in Penang, and his good work would never be forgotten.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Graft trial of Spanish PM's ex-top aide begins
Article:
MADRID: A corruption trial of Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez's former right-hand man that has rocked the government opened on Tuesday, with the defendant's ex-partner among the first witnesses to testify.
The trial of Jose Luis Abalos, a former Socialist heavyweight and transport minister who helped propel Sanchez to power in 2018, is the first of several corruption affairs shaking the fragile minority coalition to reach trial.
The case has embarrassed Sanchez, who came to power vowing to clean up Spanish politics after ousting a conservative Popular Party (PP) government in a no-confidence vote over its own graft scandal.
Abalos and his former adviser Koldo Garcia are suspected of having pocketed kickbacks for handing out public contracts worth millions of euros for sanitary equipment during the Covid-19 pandemic.
The Supreme Court in Madrid will judge them for alleged bribery, embezzlement, influence peddling, membership of a criminal organisation and misuse of confidential information. The men deny the charges.
Abalos gazed at the proceedings with apparent calm as the first testimonies were read out, an AFP journalist saw.
Next to him, Garcia kept looking at the floor, holding his head in his hand.
Prosecutors want 24 years in jail for Abalos, portraying him as the mastermind of a scheme of illicit enrichment.
They have called for a 19-year term for Garcia, who they say was a key intermediary.
They have argued that both men had abused their government positions and contacts to favour businessman Victor de Aldama, who has already admitted his role in the vast and complex affair.
The Socialists have sought to distance themselves from the case.
Deputy Prime Minister Carlos Cuerpo on Monday urged the court to act "forcefully" against Abalos, saying the party felt "outrage."
But PP spokesman Juan Bravo said Abalos was Sanchez's "friend and quite possibly his cover-up man", although the prime minister has said he knew nothing about his former minister's personal life.
More than 75 witnesses and about 20 experts are to testify during the proceedings, which are due to run through April.
Among the 11 witnesses due to speak on Tuesday is Jessica Rodriguez, an ex-partner of Abalos who was allegedly given jobs in public companies and earned a salary without showing up for work.
The investigation also appears to have ensnared Abalos's successor in the powerful post of Socialist organisation secretary, Santos Cerdan.
Caught up in another case of suspected corruption for public works contracts, he has been forced to step down from the key party position.
The fall of Abalos and Cerdan – two of Sanchez's closest allies – has embarrassed the Socialists and shaken its government with far-left junior coalition partners Sumar.
Recordings of private conversations between the accused, leaked to the media, frequently mention cash and prostitutes, dealing another blow to the Socialists who have long championed women's rights.
Separate corruption investigations into Sanchez's wife Begona Gomez and his brother David, who faces trial later this year, have piled further pressure on the government, one of few leftist administrations in Europe.
The PP and far-right opposition party Vox have called for Sanchez's resignation and early elections, saying the scandals expose systemic Socialist corruption that reaches the premier himself.
Sanchez has always denied any illegal funding of his party and rebuffed calls for polls before the next scheduled general election, due in 2027.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rafizi treading Azmin path, warns PKR branch chief
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli's recent criticisms of both PKR and the government mirror actions once associated with former party deputy president Datuk Seri Mohamed Azmin Ali, a party leader has claimed.
Pasir Mas PKR branch chief Mohd Izhan Ramli said Rafizi's tone, approach and pattern of public statements have gone beyond ordinary internal criticism, instead reflecting developments that previously led to internal fractures within the party.
He said Rafizi, a former PKR deputy president, had previously assured party leaders and delegates that he would not undermine the party from within or betray the trust placed in him, particularly in relation to prime minister and party president Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
"However, those assurances now appear hollow when statements are made openly in a tone that seems to belittle and challenge the party's own leadership," he said in a statement today.
Izhan said the issue was no longer about differences in opinion, but about the direction Rafizi himself appeared to be taking.
He drew parallels with Azmin's trajectory in the past, which he said began with open criticism before evolving into narratives that questioned party leadership and eventually led to divisions that had a significant impact on the party.
Izhan cautioned against repeating what he called a damaging chapter in the party's history, warning that trust placed by party members and the public should not be undermined by personal interests or short-term political calculations.
"Let history not record that another leader entrusted with responsibility ends up betraying the reform agenda as an 'Azmin Ali 2.0'. The struggle is bigger than any individual.
"It should not be shaped by those who attempt to bend it to their own will," he added.
Earlier, PKR secretary-general Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh said calls for
action against Rafizi
would be referred to the party's disciplinary board.
This followed calls from several PKR state leaders after his sharp criticisms of both the party and the government, warning that his remarks could spark unnecessary controversy and propagate misleading narratives, potentially undermining government effectiveness and party stability.
Rafizi, a former economy minister, on Saturday defended his criticisms, saying his remarks were intended to correct flaws rather than undermine the party.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WFH push could cut fuel use by 30pct, ease subsidy burden: Economist
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: It is positive that Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has reassured Malaysians there will be no oil supply crisis, said economist Dr Geoffrey Williams.
But stronger conservation measures are still needed to manage fuel consumption and subsidy costs, he added
Williams said petrol and diesel use should be reduced and managed more efficiently to stretch supplies and curb the rising subsidy bill.
"This is why WFH (work from home) for both public and private sector is essential. This could cut 25–30 per cent on petrol consumption helping to spread out supplies and cutting subsidies by RM1 billion," he told Business Times.
Williams warned that if fuel subsidy spending rises to RM60 billion, it would become a major fiscal burden, exceeding the RM46.5 billion allocated for public healthcare.
He said such an increase, if not budgeted, would force the government to consider spending cuts, project delays, higher taxes or increased borrowing.
"Petrol and diesel prices in Malaysia are subsidised so there should be no inflationary pressure from that.
"Import prices may be affected because higher oil prices push up prices of imports. However the ringgit is strong and so import prices are in check by that.
"The danger is that inflationary expectations are being pushed by business complaining that their costs are rising, actually they are not."
Williams added that the current situation should be used as a catalyst for long-term reforms.
But the immediate priority should be introducing WFH arrangements across the private sector and state governments, he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: U Mobile kicks off 5G rollout with TM under three-year wholesale deal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: U Mobile Sdn Bhd has kicked off work with Telekom Malaysia Bhd following a three-year 5G wholesale agreement, marking a key step in supporting the latter's network rollout.
The agreement, secured after a structured evaluation process, will see U Mobile support TM's 5G rollout under a multi-operator core network arrangement as part of its convergence strategy.
"We are on track to deliver the 5G network to TM," chief technology officer Woon Ooi Yuen said after unveiling the company's Ultra 5G solutions portfolio.
TM has exercised its contractual rights to terminate its wholesale 5G access agreement with Digital Nasional Bhd, subject to regulatory approvals.
The partnership is expected to support TM's 5G rollout, although specific timelines have yet to be disclosed.
Woon said U Mobile's 5G standalone network has achieved 80 per cent population coverage as at March and is expanding its fibre offerings to meet rising demand.
"We saw the need for fibre propositions for businesses, which is why we are expanding our coverage this year with partners," he said.
He added the expansion builds on U Mobile's existing reach of about 25 million premises passed.
"With this partnership, we will have over 350,000 customers across key market centres nationwide, a significant leap from last year.
"With this in place, we will have full coverage to support businesses across the country," he added.
U Mobile's Ultra 5G portfolio features consumer and enterprise products designed to deliver smarter, safer and prioritised connectivity experiences.
The portfolio includes features such as priority network access, integrated intelligent security, premier roaming and Ultra 5G Reserve, powered by its next-generation AI-driven 5G network.
Chief consumer business officer Navin Manian said users are increasingly demanding reliable connectivity in high-density locations such as stadiums, transport hubs, financial districts and commercial areas.
"With the rise of cyber threats and scams, security is a key concern.
"The new suite of prepaid and postpaid plans sets a new standard by offering more data, higher speeds, enhanced security and greater reliability, both locally and abroad," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ministry to roll out 5,000 projects to boost urban well-being
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: More than 5,000 public infrastructure projects will be rolled out nationwide this year to upgrade public amenities, Housing and Local Government Minister Nga Kor Ming said.
"This year, the ministry will carry out over 5,000 public infrastructure projects, including upgrades to food courts, public toilets, recreational parks and community halls," he said when officiating the National-Level International Day of Happiness 2026 celebration here today.
"These will improve the well-being of the people and public amenities," he added.
Nga said the initiatives are part of several priorities set by the ministry to strengthen sustainable urban development.
He said these include advancing smart city and digital governance, improving the quality of public infrastructure, strengthening waste management and the circular economy, as well as enhancing urban safety and resilience.
On waste management, Nga said the ministry is targeting the development of 50 smart recycling centres this year, in addition to the existing 133 drive-through recycling centres.
"We are targeting 50 smart recycling centres, on top of 133 existing drive-through facilities.
"We do not own the earth, we only borrow it from our children. So sustainability must be part of how we manage our cities," he added.
He also highlighted efforts to address flooding issues, noting that there are currently 1,450 flood hotspots nationwide.
"Last year, we managed to resolve 145 hotspots, which is about 10 per cent. With continued efforts, flash floods in urban areas could be addressed within five to six years," he said.
He said these efforts are aimed at ensuring cities remain safe, sustainable and conducive to the well-being of the people.
He added that the government is also placing greater emphasis on wellbeing as part of development, in line with the Madani framework.
Citing the Ipsos Happiness Report 2026, Nga said 80 per cent of Malaysians reported feeling happy, higher than the global average of nearly 70 per cent. Malaysia also recorded an improved score of 6.05 in the World Happiness Report, up from 5.96 previously.
To strengthen the measurement of wellbeing, Nga said urban happiness assessments have been integrated with the Malaysia Happiness Index (MHI), developed by the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM).
This ensures a more comprehensive assessment based on both national data and urban-level indicators.
Nga also urged the public to use digital platforms responsibly, in line with this year's theme, "Bijak Digital, Cipta Bahagia Bersama".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Central banks risk losing another battle by trying to win the last war
Article:
ON the face of it, the Middle East energy crisis is precisely the sort of external shock that central banks should "look through." But there are growing reasons why that may not happen this time around.
The US-Israeli war on Iran that began on Feb 28 has sent Brent crude up about 50 per cent to US$108.50 a barrel, raising the risk of an inflationary spike globally.
In theory, central banks should avoid changing policy in relation to this event unless it's clear that it will persist or boost inflation expectations long term.
The Bank for International Settlements – a group that promotes financial stability among central banks – has urged policymakers not to rush to action, calling the current crisis a textbook case of when to "look through" a supply shock.
Central banks may not heed this advice.
They are still stinging from the criticism – fair or not – that they moved too slowly after Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, mistakenly labelling elevated inflation "transitory" only to see it remain above target for years.
On top of this, the claim that inflation expectations in developed markets are "well anchored" – something central bankers have long reiterated – is now questionable.
The world has witnessed a succession of "black swan" events in recent years that risk structurally elevating inflation expectations, leading to an upward spiral of ever higher prices and greater wage demands.
First, there was the Covid-19 pandemic – supposedly a once-in-a-century shock – that led to a significant jump in prices as snarled supply chains were coupled with a stimulus-driven demand surge.
That was swiftly followed – and exacerbated – by a global energy jolt caused by Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine.
Now that conflict has been compounded by a fresh shock: the war in the Middle East.
Amid all this, the globalised trading system that suppressed inflation for decades has been fractured by US President Donald Trump's tariff wars.
Put that all together, and it starts to look like policymakers won't be able to sit on their hands for long – whether or not that's the appropriate choice.
The Bank of England, Federal Reserve and European Central Bank all held rates steady at their most recent policy meetings.
But there was little indication that they would be "looking through" the current energy crisis. Instead, the communication seemed designed to send the opposite message.
"We're well aware of the performance of inflation over the last few years and how a series of shocks have interrupted progress that we've made over time," Fed Chairman Jerome Powell said last month.
The BoE's policy meeting minutes from March 19 warned that "households and businesses could have a heightened sensitivity to any new inflationary shock, following successive negative supply shocks in recent times."
And the ECB said on the same day, "If persistent, higher energy prices may lead to a broader increase in inflation through indirect and second-round effects, a situation which requires close monitoring."
Central banks may feel compelled to act unless the Strait of Hormuz, the global energy artery currently blockaded by Iran, fully reopens soon.
[[nid:1412301]]
But recent history should give policymakers pause for thought.
Alan Taylor, an external member of the BoE's Monetary Policy Committee, recently discussed a thought experiment he and his colleagues conducted, asking what central bankers might have done from 2020 onwards if they were solely focused on bringing inflation back to target, with no concern for the knock-on growth effects.
In his scenario, UK rates would have exceeded 10 per cent in 2023 instead of peaking at 5.25 per cent, and would still be around seven per cent today, triggering a deep recession … and inflation still would have hit seven per cent.
That's just one scenario, but the situation today calls for caution for other reasons as well.
For one, the starting point for rates is quite different than it was after the pandemic. Back then, interest rates were close to zero. Today, the Fed's policy rate is 3.5-3.75 per cent, and the BoE's is 3.75 per cent.
Both have indicated they view their respective rates as somewhat restrictive. The ECB has signalled readiness to hike its two per cent key rate to combat rising inflation risks.
The lesson from the post-pandemic inflation surge is not necessarily to act faster, but to communicate better.
There were errors back then, particularly the insistence that the leap in prices was "transitory", but central banks are clearly being more careful about the projections they make this time around.
Both the ECB and BoE have introduced scenarios showing what could happen to the economy under different conditions.
In its annual Stress Test Scenarios, published in February, the Fed used a 2.2 per cent inflation projection for its baseline and a much lower one per cent for its severely adverse scenario, which typically models a demand-shock recession.
They would presumably look very different if conducted now.
The ECB's baseline puts inflation at two per cent next year, its adverse scenario projects 2.1 per cent, while its severe scenario is 4.8 per cent.
Only the latter signals the need for rate rises. The BoE will publish its equivalents at the end of April.
If underpinned by an unwavering commitment to deliver the inflation target – not now but over the medium term – such scenarios could help anchor inflation expectations without central bankers having to rapidly pull the policy lever.
They should also be wary of overreacting to credibility concerns. By responding more nimbly to the situation at hand – rather than trying to fix mistakes made in the past – policymakers could actually enhance their reliability.
Regarding the BoE, David Aikman, director at the UK's National Institute of Economic and Social Research, recently said:
"Credibility is not demonstrated by reacting mechanically to a number the Bank cannot control. It is demonstrated by being clear about what the Bank can control, what it is watching, and what it will do if the situation deteriorates."
Ultimately, if central bankers focus on trying to win the last war instead of responding to the reality of the current one, they risk being accused of making a major policy mistake yet again.
* The writer is  former head of communications at the Bank of England and a former senior editor at Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: FMM urges immediate government action over Hormuz disruption
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Federation of Malaysian Manufacturing (FMM) has called for immediate government intervention to address supply chain disruptions caused by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
A survey by FMM found that nine in 10 respondents are already affected or expect to be affected within four weeks.
FMM said raw material shortages, rising logistics costs and tightening diesel supply are threatening production continuity across sectors producing food, household goods, packaging, chemicals and consumer products.
"This is not a situation that companies can manage through tighter margins or operational adjustments. Production lines are at risk of stoppage, export orders are being cancelled and the financial capacity of manufacturers to sustain operations is under direct and accelerating pressure.
"Government intervention is required now, not when conditions deteriorate further," FMM president Jacob Lee Chor Kok said in a statement.
He said even if the conflict ends immediately, lagging effects on freight rates, raw material restocking cycles, insurance pricing and contract renegotiations would continue to weigh on operations for months.
FMM has flagged production stoppages as the most immediate risk due to a lack of critical inputs.
Its survey showed that 69.5 per cent of companies expect raw material shortages within four weeks, while 8.2 per cent have less than two weeks of stock for critical materials.
"These buffers are being depleted without replenishment, as extended lead times and disrupted shipping pipelines have broken normal restocking cycles," it said.
Among the affected supply chains are specialty chemicals used in electronics manufacturing, surface treatment and industrial coatings, which are facing reduced availability and longer lead times.
Aluminium, steel intermediates and copper-based materials transiting through affected routes are also subject to shipment delays and spot price renegotiation.
FMM said 67.3 per cent of companies expect production disruptions within one month, reflecting how material shortages will translate into reduced output, suspended product lines and unfulfilled domestic and export orders.
"Many of the manufacturers affected supply goods directly into the domestic consumer market, including food products, personal care items, household chemicals and packaging," it said.
Energy costs have also risen sharply across sectors. About 48.6 per cent of companies reported increases of between 10 per cent and 30 per cent, while 21.8 per cent reported increases of between 30 per cent and 50 per cent. A further 11.8 per cent reported increases exceeding 50 per cent.
The most affected sectors are those with high industrial diesel intensity, including ceramics, quarrying, glass manufacturing, heat-intensive food processing and marine logistics.
"Logistics costs have risen to levels that manufacturers can no longer absorb within existing export contracts. Some 52.7 per cent of companies reported freight and logistics cost increases of between 20 per cent and 50 per cent, while a further 17.7 per cent reported increases exceeding 50 per cent," it said.
FMM said 48.2 per cent of companies have already reduced output or suspended product lines in response to current conditions.
The federation proposed several measures, including tax deductions under the Income Tax Act 1967 for freight surcharges, war risk insurance premiums, vessel rerouting charges, demurrage fees and storage costs linked to the disruption.
It also urged the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry to expand the Sistem Kawalan Diesel Bersubsidi to include fuel-intensive industrial users such as ceramics, quarrying, glass manufacturing, heat-intensive food processing and marine logistics.
In addition, FMM called for Petroliam Nasional Bhd to prioritise domestic allocation of naphtha, liquefied natural gas and sulphur for downstream manufacturers during the crisis period.
"A formal domestic priority allocation framework with a defined review period and a clear sunset provision should be established through a joint directive from the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry and the Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability Ministry," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Perhilitan seizes RM36mil in wildlife parts
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Wildlife and National Parks Department's (Perhilitan) found a cache of animal parts worth more than RM36 million at a house in Taman Johor Jaya here.
A raid on Saturday led to the seizure of thousands of suspected wildlife derivatives, believed to be linked to illegal trafficking activities.
The occupant, a Vietnamese man who failed to produce any valid permits for possession of the items, was detained.
Perhilitan director-general Datuk Abdul Kadir Abu Hashim said the premises was inspected in the presence of the suspect, revealing a haul of wildlife contraband: 602 suspected python gall bladders, 420 items believed to be bear bile, 107 compressed pieces suspected to be tiger parts, 135 compressed pieces thought to be from primates, and 191 segments suspected to be python reproductive organs.
All items were confiscated and taken to the Johor Perhilitan's office for further investigation.
Abdul Kadir said the total estimated value of the seizure stood at RM36,842,325.
The case is being investigated under the Wildlife Conservation Act 2010 (Act 716), specifically Sections 60 and 68 for possession of wildlife parts without valid authorisation.
If convicted, offences involving protected wildlife carry fines of up to RM50,000 or jail terms of up to three years, or both.
However, stricter penalties apply to fully protected species such as tigers and bears.
Under Subsection 68(2)(c), offenders face fines of no less than RM150,000 for each part and imprisonment of up to 15 years.
Datuk Abdul Kadir warned that Perhilitan would continue to take firm action against those involved in wildlife crimes.
Malaysia has long been identified as both a transit and destination point for the illicit wildlife trade, with demand driven by traditional medicine and the black market.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CIQ congestion causes lorries to wait for hours at Tanjung Kupang
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Facilities for heavy vehicle inspections at the Customs, Immigration and Quarantine (CIQ) Complex in Tanjung Kupang, Iskandar Puteri, are struggling to cope with the thousands of lorries entering Singapore via the Linkedua route.
The congestion, which has been ongoing since mid-February, has caused anxiety among lorry drivers who have been dealing with long waiting times.
Lorry driver Aidil Osman, 34, said each lorry entering Singapore must pass through a scanning machine, which takes nearly five minutes per vehicle.
However, only one machine is currently available, causing long queues.
"Today, I arrived at the CIQ at 3am and the congestion stretched nearly 500m.
"I waited until 6am to clear the inspection, especially to enter the scanning machine, and at times the congestion can last up to four hours.
"This causes difficulties for drivers who have to wait for long periods, in addition to disrupting delivery or collection schedules in Singapore. Some drivers are forced to spend the night in their lorries when delivery schedules cannot be met within the stipulated time, exceeding a company's operating hours. We hope this matter will be given attention," he said.
Logistics company owner Machap Andi, 45, said facilities at the CIQ, particularly the scanning machines, need to be increased to address the issue.
"They are not objecting to inspections, but hope the facilities can accommodate the number of heavy vehicles entering Singapore.
"Moreover, diesel prices have increased, adding to the burden on logistics operators. The route is used not only by lorries from Johor, but also from other parts of the country. Facilities should take this into account," he said.
Johor Lorry Operators Association (JTA) president Chai Pei Yoon said the association had also received complaints from operators and drivers.
"We understand the problem has persisted since the Chinese New Year celebrations, with congestion for heavy vehicles being particularly severe between Tuesday and Thursday. About 3,000 lorries enter Singapore via Linkedua daily, so we hope the facilities will be improved to ensure smoother traffic flow," he said.
Efforts to obtain comments from the state Public Works, Transport, Infrastructure and Communications Committee chairman Mohamad Fazli Mohamad Salleh have yet to receive a response.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dr M denies causing Malay divisions, blames Umno changes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Former prime minister Tun Dr Mahathir Mohamad has denied accusations that he caused divisions among Malays, saying the split was driven by changes within Umno.
In a statement today, Dr Mahathir said he left the party after it deviated from its original struggle, alleging it had shifted towards pursuing wealth rather than championing the interests of the nation, religion and race.
He said he later formed Bersatu to provide an alternative platform for Malays disillusioned with Umno, with the aim of maintaining unity under a single party.
However, he noted that many Umno members chose to remain, while others who left did not join Bersatu, resulting in continued fragmentation.
"As a result, Bersatu was unable to replace Umno as the main Malay party, winning only 13 seats in the 14th general election," he said.
Dr Mahathir acknowledged that divisions among Malays persist, but stressed that unity rather than any single party has always been the source of strength, citing how Malays once rallied together under one organisation to achieve political success.
He added that efforts to establish a broader "big umbrella" platform were aimed at uniting Malays under one organisation to strengthen their position in future general elections.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police seek public help to locate missing woman
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: Police are seeking public assistance to locate a woman who was reported missing after leaving her home on Thursday (April 2).
Seberang Perai Selatan (SPS) Police chief Supt Jay January Siowou said the 38-year-old woman, G. Veaknes, was reported missing by her family.
"Anyone with information on her whereabouts is urged to contact Nibong Tebal Police Station chief Insp Mohd Muzamir Ghazali at 017-5100056, or to notify the nearest police station," he said in a statement today. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Arrest warrants issued against Daim Zainuddin's sons
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The magistrate's court today issued arrest warrants against two sons of the late Tun Daim Zainuddin for failing to comply with a notice by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) to appear in court to face charges.
The duo are Muhammed Amir Zainuddin, 31, and Muhammed Amin Zainuddin, 28.
Magistrate Illi Marisqa Khalizan also issued the same warrant against businesswoman Josephine Premla, 65.
The court made the decision after finding there were grounds to believe that all three individuals had absconded.
"I am satisfied that the named individuals had knowledge of the notice issued by MACC, yet they failed to appear.
"The prosecution's application has merit, and therefore the arrest warrants are issued," she said.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Train driver killed, 27 people injured as French TGV collides with truck
Article:
LILLE: The driver of a high-speed TGV train was killed and 27 people injured in France today when the train collided with a truck, officials said.
The accident occurred at a level crossing between the towns of Bethune and Lens in the northern French region of Pas-de-Calais at around 7:00am, said rail operator SNCF.
"I am heading to the scene with the chief executive of the SNCF, Jean Castex," Transport Minister Philippe Tabarot said on X.
Neither the SNCF nor the prefecture were able to provide details of the circumstances of the accident.
The SNCF said that rail services would be suspended between Bethune and Lens until at least late today.
In France, serious accidents involving high-speed rail lines are rare compared to traditional railways. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman charged over offensive posts on Malaysian, Brunei royal institutions
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A single mother pleaded not guilty at the Sessions court today to two counts of making and initiating offensive communications online last month.
Nurul Huda Mohd Bajuri, 49, made the plea after the charges were read before Judge Mohd Azhar Othman.
On the first count, she is accused of knowingly creating and initiating an offensive communication on the TikTok account 'ratunurutriratubalqis'.
For the second charge, she allegedly made an offensive post on the Instagram account 'ratunurutriratubalqis' which contained derogatory remarks against the Sultans of Malaysia and Brunei.
The posts were read by a man at an apartment in Sri Larkin B, Jalan Tun Abdul Razak here, at 4pm on March 27, who lodged a police report.
LINK: https://www.nst.com.my/news/nation/2026/04/1407614/cops-arrest-3-including-teenager-over-tiktok-post-insulting-johor
The charges were framed under Section 233(1)(a) of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, punishable under Section 233(2) of the same Act. It carries a maximum fine of RM500,000, a jail term of up to two years, or both, upon conviction.
An additional fine of RM5,000 may be imposed for each day the offence is continued after conviction.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Mohamad Afdhallan Syafiq Azmi requested bail at RM15,000 for both charges with two local sureties.
However, defence counsel Nurul Hidayah Mohammad Asri pleaded for a lower amount, stating that the accused is a single mother supporting a 16-year-old child and earns RM3,000 as an advisor.
The court granted bail at RM8,000 for both charges with one local surety.
Additional conditions include surrendering her passport to the court, reporting to the nearest police station once a month, and refraining from contacting prosecution witnesses until the case is concluded.
The court fixed May 8 for mention.
Last Tuesday, three individuals, including a 17-year-old, were detained in Taman Saujana to assist in investigations into TikTok content containing elements of sedition, defamation, and insults against Islam, the Johor royal institution, the Johor police chief, and the prime minister.
The two other suspects were released and will serve as prosecution witnesses.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Corruption rooted in systemic weaknesses, not just crime, says Azam Baki
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Corruption in Malaysia is not merely a legal offence but a consequence of deeper systemic weaknesses in governance.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said while laws are necessary to punish offenders, only strong integrity embedded within institutions and society can effectively prevent corruption.
Drawing on more than 42 years in anti-corruption work, he said the nature of corruption has evolved significantly, from small-scale individual offences to complex, cross-border financial crimes involving sophisticated networks and technology.
"Corruption is not just a breach of the law. It stems from weaknesses in governance systems that create space and opportunity for it to occur," he said in his keynote address at the Wacana Ilmiah Perdana at the Kuala Lumpur World Trade Centre today.
He said Malaysia is now at a critical stage where strengthening governance and institutional integrity must become central to national development, particularly as the country matures economically.
He also stressed the need to move beyond reactive enforcement, noting that relying solely on investigations after offences occur would leave authorities perpetually behind.
"Without comprehensive and effective prevention, we will always be one step behind," he said.
To address this, the MACC has adopted a broader strategy through its three-pronged approach of enforcement, prevention and effective management, alongside a shift towards risk-based and data-driven methods.
This includes developing corruption risk registers and using data analytics to identify high-risk sectors such as government procurement, enforcement agencies and public funds.
"The goal is not only to punish corruption, but to build systems where corruption becomes difficult to occur," he said.
He said leadership remains the most decisive factor in determining whether anti-corruption efforts succeed.
"If leadership is firm, clear and uncompromising, reform can take place even if the system is not yet perfect," he said, adding that strong political will is essential for meaningful governance reforms.
He cited the National Anti-Corruption Strategy (NACS) 2024–2028 as a key framework to strengthen integrity, governance and accountability across sectors.
Beyond enforcement, Azam highlighted the role of education in shaping values and building a corruption-resistant society, stressing that integrity must be cultivated from a young age through schools, universities and institutional culture.
In his welcoming remarks, Asia e University founding president Professor Dato' Dr Ansary Ahmed echoed this view, saying efforts to combat corruption must go beyond enforcement to include value-based education, character development and ethical leadership.
He said universities play a critical role in producing not only competent graduates, but individuals with strong principles and accountability.
"Without integrity, power can be abused. Without ethics, systems can collapse," he said.
Azam also warned that public trust remains a major challenge in the current information landscape, where perception can form faster than facts. He stressed that trust must be built through consistent, transparent actions rather than statements alone.
"Corruption will not end through fear of punishment, but through the courage to choose integrity," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Overnight attacks kill children in Ukraine, Russia
Article:
MOSCOW: Two boys were killed in Russia and Ukraine overnight, officials said Tuesday, as the two sides exchanged latest strikes more than four years after Moscow sent troops into its neighbour.
Moscow has been launching drones and missiles at Ukraine almost nightly throughout its offensive – the bloodiest conflict in Europe since World War II.
Ukraine has stepped up attacks on Russian energy infrastructure in recent weeks in a bid to reduce Moscow's earnings from oil exports, as the Middle East war pushes up prices.
As a result of the latest strikes, in Russia a boy born in 2014 was among three people killed in the Vladimir region after a drone struck a residential building, the regional governor said.
In Ukraine, an 11-year-old boy died and five others were wounded when a house caught fire as a result of a drone strike in the Dnipropetrovsk region, the head of the regional military administration said.
In Russia's Vladimir region, two adults and their son, born in 2014, were killed, governor Alexander Avdeev said on Telegram, adding that the couple's five-year-old daughter was in hospital with burns.
According to the Russian defence ministry quoted by media, Russia shot down 45 Ukrainian drones over the country overnight.
In Ukraine, "the enemy attacked four districts of the region more than 10 times with drones," Oleksandr Ganzha, the head of the regional military administration said on Telegram.
A house caught fire while three other homes and a vehicle were damaged in the Synelnykove district, Ganzha said.
"An 11-year-old boy was killed," he added, saying two women and a man were hospitalised.
In other areas, strikes sparked fires and damaged an administrative building and power lines, wounding two men.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia used ties with Iran to unblock vessels in Strait of Hormuz - Anwar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia has leveraged its trusted ties with Iran to help resolve the blockage of Malaysian commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said.
Anwar said his close interactions with Iranian leaders allowed him to convey Malaysia's concerns directly and secure assurances for prompt action.
"Several of our major vessels were stranded following Iran's temporary closure of the strait in response to actions by the United States and Israel.
"I contacted President Masoud Pezeshkian directly to inform him of the difficulties caused by this situation.
"He immediately instructed that the matter be addressed and assured that corrective measures would be taken without delay," he said.
He made the remarks during a discussion session at the Kuala Lumpur–Ankara Dialogue 2026 here today.
Anwar said that such diplomatic interventions are only possible through a high level of trust, but added that resolving broader re gional issues still requires cooperation from the international community.
"At the same time, we respect Iran's position and therefore do not fully support calls to reopen the Strait unilaterally without considering wider issues in the Middle East."
He added that Malaysia's approach combines diplomacy with a deep understanding of regional complexities, reflecting the importance of maintaining dialogue with all stakeholders to safeguard trade and ensure regional stability.
Earlier, it was reported that a
Malaysian‑owned ship
has transited the Strait of Hormuz, the foreign ministry said, weeks after the United States‑Israeli war with Iran began on Feb 28.
Wisma Putra in a statement said the positive outcome follows high-level diplomatic engagements.
Seven Malaysia-flagged vessels had been stuck in the strait.
Three vessels are owned by Petronas, two by MISC Bhd and one each by Sapura Energy and Shapadu Corporation Sdn Bhd.
Yesterday, the Iranian embassy in Malaysia said a
Malaysian vessel's passage through
the Strait of Hormuz highlighted strong bilateral ties.
In a post shared on its official X account, the embassy reaffirmed Teheran's commitment to its long-standing partnership with Malaysia.
The Strait of Hormuz is the world's most critical oil chokepoint, connecting the Persian Gulf with the Gulf of Oman.
Approximately 20 to 30 per cent of global oil consumption passes through this narrow waterway daily, making it a vital artery for global energy and economic security.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: No criminal elements in UPSI bowling centre storeroom death, say police
Article:
TANJUNG MALIM: Police have confirmed that no criminal elements were involved in the death of a 53-year-old man at a bowling centre at Universiti Pendidikan Sultan Idris (UPSI) early today.
Muallim district police chief Superintendent Khalid Mohamad said the victim, a local man who worked as a cleaner at the premises, was found unconscious in a utility room.
"At 1.57am, the district police headquarters operations room received information via the NG999 system regarding the incident.
"A team of policemen was immediately dispatched to the location.
"Upon arrival, personnel from the Health Ministry and the Fire and Rescue Department were already at the scene," he said in a statement today.
He added that medical personnel administered cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) for 15 minutes, but the man was pronounced dead at 2.23am.
"Initial investigations at the scene found no signs of foul play or suspicious injuries on the victim.
"The body was taken to Slim River Hospital for a post-mortem, which confirmed the cause of death as sudden cardiac arrest.
"The case has been classified as sudden death, with no criminal elements involved," he said.
He also advised the public against speculating on the case and urged them to respect the privacy of the victim's family.
Meanwhile, UPSI's Corporate Communications Division extended its deepest condolences to the family of the late Mohd Radzi Ariffin, who died earlier today.
The statement said Mohd Radzi was an employee of a private company operating at the UPSI Bowling Arena.
"UPSI will assist the family in managing the funeral arrangements once the post-mortem process is completed," it added.
Earlier, it was reported that the man died after being
trapped in a locked storeroom
at the university's sports facility. He was later found in a cleaning equipment storeroom at the university's sports complex.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department operations assistant director Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said the department received an emergency call at 1.57am requesting assistance from police to gain entry into the locked room.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chef pleads guilty to raping girlfriend's daughter
Article:
SEREMBAN: A 20-year-old chef pleaded guilty at the Sessions Court here today to a charge of raping his girlfriend's daughter in February.
Accused S. Kaveendra entered the plea after the charge was read out to him before judge Datin Surita Budin.
He was accused of raping a 16-year-old girl at a house in Taman Dato Undang Haji Mohammad Sharif, Rembau, between 8am and 10am, between Feb 26 and 28.
He was charged under Section 376(1) of the Penal Code, which carries up to 20 years in jail and whipping upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Norazihah Asmuni did not offer bail, pending sentencing.
The court allowed bail at RM10,000 and fixed June 15 for the submission of a social report and sentencing.
In a separate case, a storekeeper was charged at the same court with physically sexually assaulting his 14-year-old girlfriend under a bridge in Kampung Esok, Kenaboi, Jelebu, on March 20.
Accused Muhammad Amirullah Fadly Osman, 19, pleaded not guilty.
He was charged under Section 14(a) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017, which provides for up to 20 years in jail and whipping upon conviction.
Norazihah offered bail of RM30,000 and ordered the accused not to approach the victim and to report to the nearest police station monthly until the case is concluded.
Counsel Mohd Hanif Hassan appealed for bail to be reduced to not more than RM5,000 as the accused works as a storekeeper.
The court set bail at RM9,000 and fixed June 15 for mention for submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Madani Marts are owned by entrepreneurs; Yayasan Madani licensor for branding
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Yayasan Madani has clarified that Madani Mart operates on a public-private-social partnership (PPSP) model, with outlets fully owned by entrepreneurs and no government funds involved in its implementation.
Its manager, Lutfi Mohd Ariffin, said the foundation takes serious allegations surrounding the initiative, particularly those related to sources of capital, ownership and governance.
He explained that under the PPSP model, Yayasan Madani acts as the licensor for the branding, concept and business model registered under the Intellectual Property Corporation of Malaysia (MyIPO), without any capital outlay.
"Each outlet is fully owned and operated by entrepreneurs under their respective business entities. This ensures that every Madani Mart is a locally owned business, not owned by the government or the foundation," he said.
Lutfi added that the marts operate in an open and competitive environment, with no monopoly in the appointment of suppliers or operators. Selection, he said, is based on commercial capability, operational efficiency and supply chain effectiveness.
He said the foundation is currently reviewing applications from existing retail businesses to upgrade and join the network through an onboarding process.
"Participation is open to individuals, business partnerships and community entities such as cooperatives," he said.
Lutfi said applicants seeking to become Madani Mart operators would receive various forms of support to ensure smooth and competitive operations, including access to quality branded goods at competitive prices, renovation and equipment support, POS systems, CCTV installation and staff training.
They may also participate as strategic partners in government initiatives such as Jualan Rahmah, Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (Sara) and Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR).
He said the estimated investment required to operate a Madani Mart outlet is up to RM420,000, depending on location and scale.
He added that claims that Madani Mart is a rebranding of Kedai Rakyat 1Malaysia (KR1M) are misleading and inaccurate as the model differs fundamentally and it does not involve government funding for capital expenditure (Capex) or operational expenditure (Opex).
"Instead, it is fully driven by entrepreneur participation based on market mechanisms and private ownership. Yayasan Madani acts as an ecosystem facilitator and business incubator, not a retail operator," he said.
He added that the foundation provides structured training, business mentorship and operational support, including point-of-sale (POS) systems, CCTV installation, marketing and promotional assistance, as well as continuous AI-based retail assessments.
"At the same time, the foundation acts as a safeguard to ensure that Madani Mart's social responsibility elements are upheld, including affordability, compliance with standards and access to essential goods at Rahmah prices," he said.
On governance, Lutfi said Madani Mart operations are managed by a professional team, while the Board of Trustees ensures organisational direction, integrity and compliance.
"Board members act strictly as trustees and do not hold any personal interest in Madani Mart business operations.
Yayasan Madani only holds rights to the branding, concept and business model, while outlet ownership rests entirely with entrepreneurs. This structure prevents conflicts of interest," he said.
He said all foundation funds and transactions are recorded, audited and subject to applicable laws and regulations, with no distribution of profits to trustees.
"The foundation's financial report for 2024 has been audited, while the 2025 report is currently under audit. Service fees paid by entrepreneurs are used to support ecosystem development, including training, operational systems, supply chain access and performance monitoring," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt revives plan for National Harmony Commission
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government is reviving plans to establish an independent National Harmony Commission, with the National Unity Ministry backing a renewed proposal amid growing concerns over unity-related issues.
It said the move follows a proposal by Tan Sri Lee Lam Thye and builds on earlier efforts dating back to 2015, when the National Unity Advisory Council drafted three bills on racial and religious hatred offences, national harmony and reconciliation, and the establishment of a commission.
However, in 2019, the government rejected two of the bills due to overlaps with existing laws and constitutional provisions, including Article 8 on non-discrimination.
While the commission proposal was agreed to in principle, it later stalled over jurisdictional concerns involving agencies such as the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) and the police.
The government subsequently decided in April 2020 not to proceed.
The ministry said efforts are now being revived through the National Unity and Integration Department, with stakeholder engagement sessions already under way since early April.
"The proposed commission will prioritise prevention and reconciliation through early intervention, mediation and constructive dispute resolution, rather than punitive measures alone," the ministry said.
If established, the commission will be empowered to investigate matters affecting harmony, summon relevant parties, issue recommendations, facilitate mediation and publish reports, subject to national security considerations.
It is also expected to draw on international models, including Singapore's Maintenance of Religious Harmony Act (MHRA), as well as frameworks used by the Australian Human Rights Commission (AHRC) and Zimbabwe's National Peace and Reconciliation Commission (NPRC).
kw: National Harmony Commission, unity, inter-ethnic relations, inter-religious harmony, JPNIN, mediation, reconciliation, legislation, nst, news, Malaysia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Defence Ministry launches autism support programme for children of personnel
Article:
LUMUT: The Defence Ministry today launched the Malaysian Armed Forces Autism Initiative (IA-ATM), a programme aimed at providing therapy and support for children of defence personnel with autism.
The initiative is a new ministry project in collaboration with the Health Ministry, the Social Welfare Department, and the National Autism Society of Malaysia (Nasom), with funding provided by Lembaga Tabung Haji.
Its deputy minister, Adly Zahari said IA-ATM is initially being implemented at two locations, at the Community-Based Rehabilitation Organisation (PPDK) Lautan Kasih in Lumut and PPDK Kem Terendak in Melaka, involving 20 children.
"This programme focuses on children with autism aged three to 15 years who are children of defence personnel.
"They undergo 12 therapy modules specially developed by Nasom over a 12-month period.
"So far, the programme has reached its eighth month and shows positive progress among participants," he said.
He was speaking to reporters after launching the programme at PPDK Lautan Kasih at the Navy base in Lumut today.
Also present were Western Fleet Commander Admiral Datuk Baharudin Wan Md Nor and Nasom president Datuk Megat Ahmad Shahrani Megat Sharuddin.
The Nasom-developed therapy modules cost RM2,200 per participant, with the overall programme at both PPDKs amounting to RM528,000.
Adly said interactions with parents have shown notable improvements in the children's abilities.
"Progress takes time, as therapy effectiveness becomes more apparent as the children grow, but the early results are very encouraging," he said.
He added that while about 55 children in the community have been identified, only 20 were selected for the initial phase.
"Selection was carefully conducted by Nasom and PPDK to ensure the programme's effectiveness before expanding it in the future.
"Experience from this programme will guide improvements and allow more children of defence personnel to benefit," he said.
For the long term, he said the ministry plans to strengthen collaborations with government-linked companies, government-linked investment companies, and non-governmental organisations to broaden support.
"When the welfare of families is taken care of, personnel can focus fully on defending the nation," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Online gaming platforms expose children to grooming risks
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: An acquaintance that began through an online game ultimately led to requests for inappropriate images.
This was among the real situations experienced by a 12-year-old girl after she became acquainted with an unknown individual via a gaming platform.
What initially started as a casual conversation became risky when the individual began requesting inappropriate images, before it was discovered by her parents.
Situations like this are no longer isolated cases, but instead reflect an increasingly worrying reality.
The age limit of 16 is not set arbitrarily. At this stage, adolescents' cognitive and emotional development is still in a formative phase, particularly in aspects of self-control, risk assessment and decision-making ability.
This makes them more vulnerable to manipulation, exploitation and online threats compared with older individuals.
Child clinical psychologist Dr Noor Aishah Roslan said most children today already own smartphones and have access to social media from a young age, yet their level of maturity has not fully developed to assess risks.
"From a cognitive development perspective, they are still unable to distinguish whether information can be trusted.
"At the same time, their desire to socialise makes them more likely to trust others, including strangers, without realising the actual risks," she said.
According to her, among the things that most easily attract children's attention are praise, digital rewards, emotionally engaging sexual content, as well as self-harm content that goes viral on social media.
"When children interact with such content and receive praise or attention, they may share personal information, images or phone numbers without realising they are being exploited.
"Manipulative interactions such as grooming have an impact on children's psychological development.
"Short-term effects include fear, anxiety, low mood, loss of focus and social withdrawal. In the long term, it can lead to trauma, depression and tendencies towards self-harm.
"For children who are exploited sexually or financially, prolonged pressure can occur even without physical contact," she said.
She added that most children are unaware they are facing risks, as they perceive such interactions as something positive.
Factors such as fear of being scolded, embarrassment and feelings of guilt also make it difficult for them to share their experiences with parents or authorities.
In some cases, emotional manipulation does not only involve the children, but also their parents.
This indicates that existing digital safety measures are still insufficient to fully protect them.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Protesters disrupt Azam Baki's speech at public forum
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A public address by Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki at the Wacana Ilmiah Perdana was briefly disrupted by three youths who staged a protest during the event.
The incident unfolded as one of the youths suddenly stood up holding a placard and walked towards the front of the hall, drawing the attention of guests.
[[nid:1412280]]
Moments later, a second youth also rose from his seat carrying a placard.
He moved to the middle of the hall and began shouting "Tangkap Azam Baki", interrupting the event.
Organisers and auxiliary policemen quickly intervened, escorting both individuals out of the hall to restore order.
[[nid:1412279]]
A third youth, who had initially been seated with the pair, was subsequently asked to leave the venue.
He initially argued with organisers before eventually complying and exiting the hall.
The situation was brought under control shortly after, allowing the event to proceed without further disruption.
Azam delivered his address and left the venue shortly after.
It was understood that representatives from the MACC strategic communications division will lodge a police report on the matter later today.
#kw: Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission, MACC, chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam, disruption, protest, Wacana Ilmiah Perdana, speech
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia to push for South China Sea code with China - Anwar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia will push for a regional code of conduct with China to resolve disputes in the South China Sea without outside interference, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said today.
Speaking at the Kuala Lumpur–Ankara Dialogue 2026, Anwar acknowledged ongoing tensions involving China and the Philippines but stressed that Asean must take the lead in managing the disputes.
"We will press for the Code of Conduct with China, together with our Asean partners, to secure and resolve this problem without interference from other forces," he said.
Anwar made the remarks during a Q&A session moderated by ISIS Malaysia Executive Chairman, Dr Mohd Faiz Abdullah.
He was asked how Malaysia is positioned to handle a potential crisis, given that about 40 per cent of global trade passes through the Malacca Strait and tensions are rising in the South China Sea and Indian Ocean.
Anwar said the issue will be raised at the upcoming Asean leaders' meeting in the Philippines next month, where maritime stability and regional cooperation are expected to dominate discussions.
He added that safeguarding sovereignty and strategic independence is crucial, not just in the South China Sea but across wider regional waterways, including the Indian Ocean.
"That is very critical. We must be able to resolve our own problems and differences. We must uphold our rights and maintain our strategic independence."
ends./
#kw, Malaysia, News, Nst, Malaysia News, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, Nation,South China Sea, Asean, CoC
